title: Naming Acids, Indicators and Acid Reactions
subtitle: Name any acid and predict what it makes
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: investigate the correct IUPAC nomenclature and properties of common inorganic acids and bases (ACSCH067)
conduct an investigation to demonstrate the preparation and use of indicators as illustrators of the characteristics and properties of acids and bases and their reversible reactions (ACSCH101)
predict the products of acid reactions and write balanced equations to represent reactions of acids with bases, carbonates, hydrogen carbonates and reactive metals
inquiryQuestion: What is an acid and what is a base?
lessonIntent: Students can name common acids and bases by IUPAC rules, explain indicator colour change as an equilibrium, and predict and balance the products of acid reactions.
examSkill: Name acids using the binary or oxoacid pattern; apply HIn ⇌ H⁺ + In⁻ with Le Chatelier; predict salt + water (+ CO₂ or H₂) and balance the equation.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, naming acids, indicators, and predicting acid reactions. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Naming Acids, Indicators and Acid Reactions (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 6, Lesson 2)

scenes.heading: One drop tells the truth
scenes.body: A winemaker adds a pale orange drop to wine and it turns yellow. She writes 'too acidic' and adjusts the batch.
scenes.callout: How can a single weak acid read out the acidity of anything?
scenes.caption: Indicators reveal acidity at a glance because they are weak acids that change colour.

voiceover.text: OK, picture three people doing the same trick. A winemaker adds a few drops of a pale orange solution to her wine, it turns yellow, and she writes too acidic. A pharmacist dips a strip into dissolved aspirin, it turns pink, and he confirms the solution is basic. A soil scientist tests a paddock, gets orange, and prescribes lime. All three solutions looked clear before the drop went in, and all three are using the same kind of substance, a weak acid called an indicator. Today you'll learn how that colour change works, how to name any acid or base from its formula, and how to predict exactly what an acid makes when it reacts.

scenes.heading: Two naming patterns, never mixed
scenes.body: First ask: does the acid contain oxygen? That single question picks the naming rule.

bullets.text: No oxygen: a binary acid, named hydro-(element)-ic, like HCl, hydrochloric acid.
Has oxygen: an oxoacid, named from its ion. The -ate ion gives -ic acid.
The -ite ion gives -ous acid: sulfite gives sulfurous acid.

scenes.secondary: Hydro- is never used for an oxoacid. So H₂SO₄ is sulfuric acid, never hydrosulfuric, which is the name for H₂S.
scenes.callout: Oxygen present? Name it from the ion, and drop the hydro- prefix.
scenes.caption: No oxygen → hydro-...-ic acid; has oxygen → name from the ion (-ate→-ic, -ite→-ous).

voiceover.text: Let's name acids systematically. The whole thing turns on one question, does the acid contain oxygen? If it doesn't, it's a binary acid, just hydrogen and one non-metal, and you name it hydro, then the element root, then ic acid. So HCl is hydrochloric acid, HBr is hydrobromic acid. If it does contain oxygen, it's an oxoacid, and you name it from the polyatomic ion it carries. An ion ending in ate gives an acid ending in ic, so sulfate gives sulfuric acid. An ion ending in ite gives an acid ending in ous, so sulfite gives sulfurous acid. The classic exam trap, never use hydro for an oxoacid. Sulfuric acid is never hydrosulfuric, because hydrosulfuric is the name for H two S, which has no oxygen.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Binary acid: hydrogen plus one non-metal, no oxygen, like HCl.
Oxoacid: hydrogen, oxygen and another element, like H₂SO₄.
Strong bases to memorise: NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂, Ba(OH)₂.
Indicator: a weak acid whose acid and base forms are different colours.

scenes.callout: Only four strong bases. Treat every other base, including ammonia, as weak.
scenes.caption: Binary acid, oxoacid, the four strong bases, indicator.

voiceover.text: Quick vocabulary. A binary acid is hydrogen with one non-metal and no oxygen, like hydrochloric acid. An oxoacid contains hydrogen, oxygen, and another element, like sulfuric acid. For bases, there are exactly four strong ones you simply memorise, sodium hydroxide, potassium hydroxide, calcium hydroxide, and barium hydroxide. They dissociate completely, so you write a single arrow. Every other base in this module, including ammonia, sodium carbonate, and magnesium hydroxide, is weak. And an indicator is a weak acid whose acid form and conjugate base form happen to be different colours, which is what lets it report acidity. One warning, ammonia in water is not ammonium hydroxide. It accepts a proton from water, written ammonia plus water gives ammonium plus hydroxide.

scenes.heading: Indicators are weak-acid equilibria
scenes.body: An indicator sits in equilibrium HIn ⇌ H⁺ + In⁻, where the two forms are different colours.

bullets.text: Acidic solution: high H⁺ pushes the equilibrium left, so the HIn colour shows.
Basic solution: low H⁺ pulls it right, so the In⁻ colour shows.
Memorise: methyl orange red/yellow, bromothymol blue yellow/blue, phenolphthalein colourless/pink.

scenes.secondary: Phenolphthalein changes over pH 8.3 to 10, so it is colourless at pH 7, not pink. Only bromothymol blue spans neutral.
scenes.callout: Le Chatelier drives the colour: add H⁺, shift left, see the acid colour.
scenes.caption: HIn ⇌ H⁺ + In⁻: acid shifts left (acid colour), base shifts right (base colour).

voiceover.text: Now the colour change, and it's pure Le Chatelier. An indicator is a weak acid we write as H-I-n, where the intact acid form and its conjugate base In-minus are different colours. The equilibrium is H-I-n in balance with H-plus plus In-minus. In an acidic solution, hydrogen ions are high, so the system shifts left to oppose them, the H-I-n form dominates, and you see the acid colour. In a basic solution, hydroxide mops up hydrogen ions, so the system shifts right to replace them, the In-minus form dominates, and you see the base colour. Memorise three. Methyl orange goes red to yellow over pH three to four-and-a-half. Bromothymol blue goes yellow to blue over six to seven-and-a-half. Phenolphthalein goes colourless to pink over eight-point-three to ten. So phenolphthalein is colourless at pH seven, not pink, a very common slip.

diagram.beforeContent: Equilibrium shifts LEFT → HIn form → acid colour
diagram.afterContent: Equilibrium shifts RIGHT → In⁻ form → base colour

scenes.heading: Three acid reaction patterns
scenes.body: Identify the reactant type, and the products fall out automatically.

bullets.text: Acid + base → salt + water. The net ionic is H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O.
Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂. All three products, every time.
Acid + reactive metal → salt + H₂. Only metals above hydrogen react.

scenes.secondary: Build the salt by pairing the metal cation with the acid anion and balancing charges first. The gas is H₂, never H.
scenes.callout: Carbonates give CO₂; reactive metals give H₂. Don't drop the gas.
scenes.caption: Acid+base→salt+water; acid+carbonate→+CO₂; acid+reactive metal→+H₂.

voiceover.text: Acids react in three predictable patterns. One, acid plus base gives a salt and water, and the net ionic equation for a strong pair is just hydrogen ion plus hydroxide gives water. Two, acid plus a carbonate or hydrogen carbonate gives a salt, water, and carbon dioxide. The carbon dioxide appears because the carbonic acid that forms is unstable and instantly falls apart into water and CO two, which is the bubbling you see. All three products must be in your equation. Three, acid plus a reactive metal gives a salt and hydrogen gas, but only for metals above hydrogen in the activity series, so magnesium, aluminium, zinc and iron react, while copper and silver don't. To get the salt, pair the metal cation with the acid's anion and balance the charges before you balance the equation. And the gas is H two, never a lone H.

scenes.heading: Naming from formulas
scenes.question: Name and classify: (a) HNO₂, (b) H₂S, (c) Ba(OH)₂.
scenes.coachNote: Check for oxygen first, then pick the pattern.
steps: HNO₂ has oxygen → oxoacid. Ion is NO₂⁻, nitrite, ends -ite → nitrous acid (weak)
H₂S has no oxygen → binary acid → hydro + sulf + ic = hydrosulfuric acid (weak)
Ba(OH)₂ is a metal cation plus hydroxide → barium hydroxide
Barium hydroxide is one of the four strong bases
Answers: nitrous acid (weak); hydrosulfuric acid (weak); barium hydroxide (strong) ✓
scenes.caption: HNO₂ nitrous acid; H₂S hydrosulfuric acid; Ba(OH)₂ barium hydroxide.

voiceover.text: Let's name three. First H-N-O-two. It contains oxygen, so it's an oxoacid, and its ion is nitrite, which ends in ite, so the acid ends in ous, giving nitrous acid, a weak acid. Second H two S. No oxygen, so it's a binary acid, hydro plus sulf plus ic, giving hydrosulfuric acid, also weak. Notice this one really does get hydro, because it has no oxygen. Third, barium hydroxide. That's a metal cation with hydroxide, named simply as barium hydroxide, and it's one of our four strong bases, so it fully dissociates. The trick across all three is that first question about oxygen. Answer that, and the pattern picks itself.

scenes.heading: Balancing the three patterns
scenes.question: Write balanced equations: (a) HCl + CaCO₃, (b) HNO₃ + Al.
scenes.coachNote: Get the salt formula from charges, then balance. Watch the gases.
steps: (a) Acid + carbonate → salt + water + CO₂. Salt: Ca²⁺ with Cl⁻ → CaCl₂
(b) Acid + reactive metal → salt + H₂. Salt: Al³⁺ with NO₃⁻ → Al(NO₃)₃
scenes.caption: 2HCl + CaCO₃ → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂; 6HNO₃ + 2Al → 2Al(NO₃)₃ + 3H₂.

voiceover.text: Now let's balance two. Part a, hydrochloric acid with calcium carbonate, that's acid plus carbonate, so the products are a salt, water, and carbon dioxide. The salt pairs calcium two-plus with chloride, giving calcium chloride, C-a-C-l-two. Balancing gives two HCl plus calcium carbonate gives calcium chloride plus water plus carbon dioxide. All three products are there. Part b, nitric acid with aluminium, that's acid plus a reactive metal, and aluminium is above hydrogen, so it reacts to give a salt plus hydrogen gas. The salt pairs aluminium three-plus with nitrate, giving aluminium nitrate with three nitrates. Balancing the aluminiums and the hydrogens gives six nitric acid plus two aluminium gives two aluminium nitrate plus three H two. The gas is H two, never a single H.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: naming H₂SO₄ hydrosulfuric. It has oxygen, so it is sulfuric acid; hydrosulfuric is H₂S.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: assuming HF is strong because it's a binary acid like HCl. The short, strong H–F bond makes HF weak. Trap 3: leaving CO₂ out of acid plus carbonate, or writing H instead of H₂ for acid plus metal.
scenes.mistakeTag: Where naming and equation marks slip
scenes.callout: Oxoacids drop hydro-; HF is weak; never lose the gas product.
scenes.caption: H₂SO₄ is sulfuric not hydrosulfuric; HF is weak; keep CO₂ and write H₂.

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, naming sulfuric acid as hydrosulfuric. It has oxygen, so it's an oxoacid named from sulfate, giving sulfuric acid. Hydrosulfuric is reserved for H two S, which has no oxygen. Trap two, assuming hydrofluoric acid is strong just because it's a binary acid like hydrochloric. It isn't. The hydrogen fluorine bond is unusually short and strong because fluorine is tiny, so the proton is hard to release, which makes HF a weak acid. The bigger halides, hydrobromic and hydroiodic, have weaker bonds and are strong. Trap three, the gases. In acid plus carbonate, students drop the carbon dioxide and write only salt and water, but all three products must appear. And in acid plus metal, the gas is H two, a diatomic molecule, never a lone H.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: Phenolphthalein is added to three solutions: pH 5, pH 9, pH 12. What colour is each?
answerSteps: Phenolphthalein transition range is pH 8.3 to 10.0; colourless below, pink above
pH 5 is below 8.3, so HIn dominates → colourless
pH 9 is inside 8.3 to 10.0, so In⁻ dominates → pink
pH 12 is above 10.0, so In⁻ still dominates → pink (it does not return to colourless)
Answer: pH 5 colourless, pH 9 pink, pH 12 pink ✓
scenes.caption: Phenolphthalein: pH 5 colourless, pH 9 pink, pH 12 pink.

voiceover.text: Quick check. Phenolphthalein is added to three solutions, pH five, pH nine, and pH twelve. What colour is each? Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. Phenolphthalein's transition range is eight-point-three to ten, colourless below it and pink above it. At pH five we're well below the range, so the acid form dominates and it stays colourless. At pH nine we're inside the range, so the base form takes over and it turns pink. At pH twelve we're above the range, and here's the catch, it stays pink, because the base form still dominates at high pH. It does not go back to colourless. So colourless, pink, pink.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: No oxygen → binary acid, hydro-...-ic; has oxygen → oxoacid from the ion.
Strong bases are NaOH, KOH, Ca(OH)₂, Ba(OH)₂; treat the rest as weak.
Indicators are weak acids: HIn ⇌ H⁺ + In⁻, colour set by Le Chatelier.
Acid + base → salt + water; + carbonate → also CO₂; + reactive metal → also H₂.
Build the salt from ion charges, then balance, and keep the gas.
scenes.finalPrompt: Naming an acid? Ask first whether it contains oxygen.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: two naming patterns, four strong bases, indicator equilibrium, three reaction patterns, salt-first balancing.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, to name an acid, ask whether it has oxygen, no means a binary acid with the hydro-ic pattern, yes means an oxoacid named from its ion. Second, only four bases are strong, sodium, potassium, calcium and barium hydroxide, and everything else, including ammonia, is weak. Third, indicators are weak acids in the equilibrium H-I-n with H-plus and In-minus, and Le Chatelier decides the colour. Fourth, acids react in three patterns, with a base giving salt and water, with a carbonate also giving carbon dioxide, and with a reactive metal also giving hydrogen gas. And fifth, always build the salt from the ion charges first, then balance, and never drop the gas. Next lesson, we measure the heat these neutralisations release. See you there.
